data.title: Ulta Beauty: Heavy Costs Now, Stronger Tomorrow -- A Quality Diversifier
data.description:
Thesis

The Ulta Beauty, Inc. (ULTA) quarterly results just made public showed that the company is resilient even under trying circumstances and able to produce appealing income. Demand is still constant, and sales and profits have grown. Said this, the company faces significant costs.

Those who follow my analysis know that I rarely increase my equity exposure these days, but if I have to select and include stocks and/or themes that I like the most, I take the opportunity to reduce themes that have run too high and/or are more tied to hype than fundamentals. No more bets, welcome to quality opportunities. This, in my opinion, is a quality opportunity, but I'm not going too far.

Company Overview

Ulta Beauty is certainly one of the main players in the sector, perhaps the largest. It has more than 1,400 stores and a catalog that brings together more than 600 brands and a loyalty program with 45 million members. I repeat this to emphasize the number: forty-five million members.

For a retailer, this is a very important fact because it means that it is no longer necessary to "chase" customers every quarter with new offers, launches and promotions (which can erode margins). We're talking about affiliate customers.

Besides, it seems normal today, but when they started operating this way years ago, no one did it. It's about putting affordable and premium products and services like hair, eyebrows, and skincare in the same place. All in one place. A kind of world dedicated to beauty. You walk in and find everything you need. It seems trivial, but it's a unique experience, especially considering it was already being done years ago. And above all, it has improved over time and has allowed the company to create a sort of "addiction" in customers who choose to return to the stores every month, which means stable and predictable business.

Furthermore, compared to other similar sectors such as clothing, the beauty industry is experiencing steady growth (4-5% annually), with customers who, once they trust the product, are unlikely to change their habits. This creates long-term positioning, which ultimately makes all the difference.

They're making a step forward with "heavy" investments in technology. Some may not like this, but I look at the long term and have always maintained that investments bring growth, and growth, when supported by solid finances, is always welcome. In any field, including beauty. The company is investing heavily in technology, supply chain, and digital systems. We'll discuss this in the section dedicated to the recently published earnings season, but I can anticipate that these costs are clearly reflected in SG&A, which may bother some investors, who are much more concerned with margins than long-term growth. It's better to sacrifice a few points of margin today to see greater growth tomorrow. Many other companies have made a qualitative leap in this way in the past, think of Target, Home Depot, or Nike, all of which have gone through a phase of heavy investment.

Internationally, we're still in the early stages, and in my opinion, this should be viewed positively. So far, the company has focused on the American market, and it seems to have done so for the most part; tomorrow, it could focus on expanding its market to Europe, China, etc. So, what might seem like a shortcoming today should actually be seen as an option to consider for the future. I would point out, however, that something is moving; in fact, a "light" model already exists in the Middle East and Mexico.

Earnings

The quarterly results were better than expected and, in fact, satisfied investors (as can also be seen from the post-market reaction). Many people consider the post-market reaction to be unreliable, but I personally believe it's an indication of how the market has received the news. As of this writing, the stock is up almost 7% after the close of trading.

As usual, I recommend an in-depth reading in the dedicated section of Seeking Alpha, where all the relevant information is provided. In this analysis, I'll highlight what I liked and what I think performed less well than expected.

What worked

Revenues rose 12.9% to $2.86 billion, and considering that until a year ago growth was almost zero, it clearly illustrates the dramatic change in pace seen in recent months.

Almost all subcategories grew compared to last year, and by double digits.

In my opinion, Ulta's real secret is customer loyalty. They pay a huge amount of attention to this aspect and even emphasized it during the call with analysts. In my opinion, it's a key factor that makes customer relationships more stable than you might think, creating routines and stable sales over time. I'm talking about work that can't be seen in the numbers, but which is done day after day through customer relationships and which significantly strengthens sales. The advantage of having a database of this size is that you understand what really generates sales and increases customer visits, and therefore you can improve the methods to maximize it.

Margins are also following a steadily growing trend, as demonstrated by the company's public data.

In​‍​‌‍​‍‌ addition to that, the firm openly elevated its guidance with a higher top line, better "like-for-like" performance, increased operating margin, and earnings per share going up to 25.20-25.50.

What Didn't Work

Rather than "it didn't work," I should say "it was less convincing." As I said before, the company is pushing hard on long-term growth, partially sacrificing earnings and margins. In the following table, you'll notice that EPS has remained virtually unchanged from year to year.

I'm writing this in the "what didn't work" section because, objectively, it's right to report it here. Flat EPS growth, or rather, "non-growth," needs to be accounted for by an analyst like me. There's a catch. In my view, it's a sensible choice, because even if SG&A is growing more than sales (23% versus 13%), it's also true that a portion of these costs isn't "wasted expense," but investment. The company indicated in the analyst call that they will have to put their accounts in order next year, but the direction is clear: they want to invest in technology, supply chain, qualified personnel, store upgrades, and the entire digital component that is currently the true backbone of the model. So yes, in the short term, EPS is stagnant, but the key is understanding why it is stagnant. It's an effect of investments. And this completely changes the interpretation. If Ulta had declining margins because it has to cut prices or because traffic isn't holding up, it would be a very different story.

Of course, any investment can be a mistake with hindsight. I don't want to insist that any type of investment is a good thing, but it seems that in this case, the factors we have at our disposal suggest that the current investments are limited risk. Why? First of all, because demand is strong and broad; Gross margin is increasing, not decreasing, so if they had invested poorly, you would see it in the gross margin (it improved to 40.4%); Loyalty continues to strengthen; in fact, there are 46.3 million members, with greater engagement than last year; the investment is in digital and technology in general, which would generate an additional boost to sales; finally, we are not risking anything, free cash flow is there, and even with ongoing investments, the company is able to generate cash and make buybacks.

Valuation

To be direct and concise, I'll tell you that if you look at SA's quant model, the "D-" rating could clearly scare and alienate all investors. I’m not ignoring this, obviously. The fact is, it needs to be carefully contextualized, given that this rating also derives from an "automatic" comparison with traditional retail, where the industry median is around 15-17 times earnings and 10-11 times EV/EBITDA.

The company presents all the metrics at the high end of the range, it's true, but we're talking about a company that generates gross margins of 40%, has a huge loyalty moat, and earnings visibility that goes beyond the reach of the average store. So the industry comparison is fine, but it should also be taken with a grain of salt.

Looking at future valuations, Ulta isn't at a discount, but it's not out of line either. The price-to-earnings ratio is expected to decline over the next few years, from approximately 21x to 15x in 2029, a sign that earnings will resume steady growth.

My target price for the stock is $642 per share. In practice, I'm paying approximately 20.5x 2027 earnings and approximately 18x 2029 earnings. The numbers reflect everything we've discussed in the previous paragraphs (i.e., margin growth, investments, cash flow, and retention). I justify the premium with the company's greater visibility and (thanks to customer loyalty) lower cyclicality compared to other companies in the industry. I'm also taking into account that the company is beginning to consolidate its presence in other geographies, which could lead to increased sales in areas currently unexplored by the company.

Risks

I don't see any major risks on the horizon, but there are some signs that definitely need to be monitored. As I said before, SG&A is growing, and there's not much to say about that. In Q3, operating expenses grew by 23% versus about 13% of revenues. If growth were to slow in the coming quarters, margins would be impacted. However, it must also be said that management has said there will be "more discipline" in 2026, so I want to trust them and wait for the next few quarters before raising a "warning."

I would add that the beauty sector is more resilient than apparel, but it's still not immune to cracks and/or slowdowns. Consumers may arrive "tired," and some pressure on the "premium" segment is not unrealistic. At the moment, I don't see any such signs, so I wouldn't worry too much, but it's still a risk worth monitoring carefully.

Finally, debt. It's not a red flag, but I don't like the fact that it's rising due to buybacks. It's good for investments, but a little less so for buybacks, in my opinion.

Conclusion

Ulta Beauty is a stock worth adding to your portfolio because the company has a model that really works and can serve to diversify your portfolio from the usual names and sectors (like technology) that I have personally reduced in my portfolio lately. Generally speaking, I remain cautious about the market in general, but this stock could perform really well at this stage. In any case, I don't think this is the stock that will change my life tomorrow and/or bring me strong profits, but I need it today to improve sector diversification and add a business that remains stable even when the cycle doesn't go in its favor.

The question an investor should ask is: if I were to add it, how much would I invest? Personally, I would start with a cautious "starting position." I would then keep the door open to further increasing my stake if I saw ULTA controlling its costs and margins returning to normal in subsequent quarters.

data.title: Applied Digital Is Showing All The Right Signs With Its Liquid Cooling Investments
data.description:
Thesis

Applied Digital Corporation (APLD) stock has been on an upward swing since November 21st. The drop in share price in early November was something that would now be viewed as a discount. The stock has ridden on the back of a couple of bits of good news, most recently the investment into Corintis, a company that specializes in liquid cooling.

Going forward, Applied Digital still have a lot of data center construction ahead of them, so this investment couldn't have come at a better time. I also see this move fitting in nicely with the arrival of more compute-heavy GPUs like the Blackwell series. Elsewhere, Applied Digital have just completed Building 1 at their Polaris Forge 1 campus, still on track to complete the capacity rollout over the course of 2026. Now with the stock trading at around $30 per share, I still see more upside ahead for the company going into next year.

Another 50MW energized

Elsewhere, Applied Digital managed to complete Phase II at Polaris Forge 1, so that means they’ve energized the first 100 MW building. It’s a good step in the right direction, and as per my previous coverage, I estimated Applied Digital's online capacity to be valued at about $150,000 per megawatt per month once fully in service and leased based on the figure we saw from the CoreWeave (CRWV) deal. So this 100 MW should translate to about $150 million if leased at a similar price. So now, by delivering that second 50 MW phase on time, Applied Digital has brought Building 1 to its full critical IT load. That makes the first of three contracted buildings at the campus online, and another milestone is completed on the way to advancing the campus’s ultimate 400 MW capacity.

Getting the capacity online in the time frame they did also makes me bullish on how they’re able to execute these AI data center builds. We’re seeing the Polaris Forge 1 get built with quite impressive precision and speed. This kind of speed is going to be critical when rapid deployment of GPU-intensive infrastructure becomes more in demand. What fits in nicely with the projected rollout is the investment we've just seen into more advanced cooling tech. I think Applied Digital are showing all the signs to accommodate more computing power with future Blackwell adoption, as I'll explain.

A strategic investment

The latest news is that Applied Digital just announced they’ve put up $25 million in funding for Corintis. They’re a Swiss-based developer of some pretty advanced chip-cooling tech, designed specifically to boost efficiency and power density in AI-intensive data centers. The investment aligns quite well with Applied Digital’s strategy to get a stronger foothold in high-performance, AI-optimized infrastructure. And now, by backing critical innovations in power, cooling, and sustainability, they’re hoping to get a nice return from Corintis in terms of power efficiency.

You see, Corintis has a microfluidic, direct-to-chip liquid-cooling platform in development right now. So, since it's still undergoing quite a few changes, it allows cooling to be tailored specifically at the chip level to eventually be a drop-in replacement for standard cold plates for Applied Digital. We might even see it being integrated directly into GPUs. The technology has already demonstrated that it works at up to three times lower chip temperatures compared to other conventional solutions, and what's more, it's been validated by Microsoft. So Applied Digital hopes it can support higher power densities and just overall improve energy efficiency, one of their biggest challenges going forward.

The way Corintis’ cooling method works is by using extremely tiny engineered channels, which are placed directly over the hottest regions of a GPU/accelerator. So it allows coolant to absorb heat at the silicon level rather than through a large metal cold plate, which is what we usually see. Think of it as precision cooling, which can remove the heat far more efficiently to give lower chip temperatures. As a result, they can produce much higher power densities on the flip side and, of course, reduced energy waste. In terms of what Applied Digital want, I see the potential here being pretty significant. In theory, it should support much denser AI racks and cut cooling costs. It can also unlock the ability to run next-generation GPUs at full performance. Now that last point is pretty important since Blackwell use is ramping up. These kinds of GPUs can deliver some enormous compute power, but also generate massive heat and need very dense power delivery. For example, the Blackwell family can draw a hefty 1 kW per chip or even higher, so if it’s to be under full load, it could produce heat outputs that air cooling simply can’t handle reliably. I’m expecting the investment to eventually lead to a competitive edge for Applied Digital when it comes to ultra-high-density, AI-optimized data centers.

Corintis will now use the new capital to expand the U.S. presence and ramp up global manufacturing of its microfluidic cooling systems. The capital should also help them accelerate customer deployments as demand for thermal solutions intensifies. This partnership also follows a series of big milestones for Applied Digital, including long-term hyperscale leases at its Polaris Forge campuses and its multibillion-dollar partnership with Macquarie Asset Management, all events that saw significant upside to the stock.

How does this fit into the buildout

I think now is a good time to put the investment into Corintis. We could also see them pilot the microfluidic cooling technology at Polaris Forge 1 in Ellendale, potentially in later phases of Building 1 or the subsequent 50 MW buildings. I’m targeting deployment in late 2026 to early 2027. The platform is specifically designed to support next-generation Nvidia (NVDA) Blackwell GPUs and other high-density AI accelerators. So moving forward, it’s tipped to enable precise chip-level cooling that can reduce temperatures by up to 3 times compared with the cooling in Building 1 at Polaris. Full-scale integration across Polaris Forge 2 and future AI Factory campuses, which would include up to 400 MW per campus, would likely occur in 2027 to 2028 if Applied Digital decide to pursue Corintis tech further. The company is currently scaling manufacturing, and Applied Digital certifies reliability for multi-50 to 100 MW high-density racks.

We should see phased adoption dovetail with Applied Digital's broader infrastructure rollout. It will allow for staged validation without disrupting ongoing critical IT loads. Most importantly, I see the efficiency gains from lower coolant temperatures contributing meaningfully to the $2.7 billion projected lifecycle cost savings over the next 30 years.

The negatives: a premium valuation

Now, the stock has been on a pretty bullish trend this year until October. Around mid-October, the stock saw downside and eventually hit figures in the $21 range in late November. I thought at that point Applied Digital shares were extremely cheap, and the stock has already started to see recovery into the $32 range.

The company's valuation now seems quite stretched relative to the broader tech sector. It shows the rapid growth trajectory and also the market’s expectations for more AI-driven revenue. On a trailing twelve-month basis, the company now trades at a price to sales ratio of 40.9x and 29.9x on a forward basis. Both are dramatically above the sector medians of 3.67x and 3.6x, respectively. It goes to show just how aggressively investors are pricing in its projected hyperscale. Its trailing twelve-month enterprise value to sales ratio also sits very high at 55.2x. Another indication of the premium.

However, if you take a closer look into the peer group it consists of GDS Holdings (GDS), Wix.com (WIX), Akamai Technologies (AKAM), DigitalOcean (DOCN), and Kingsoft Cloud (KC) which all operate in data/cloud Infrastructure but would not be directly comparable to how Applied Digital operate in the AI/high-density data center space expect for GDS Holdings. The players I would directly compare to Applied Digital to this year would be Nebius (NBIS), Iren Limited (IREN) and CoreWeave. When that comparison is made, the multiple don't screen as expensive as they did before, as you can see in the table below.

Looking ahead

Investors should follow the construction progress a the Polaris campus quite closely. When Applied Digital managed to bring capacity online, the stock usually sees a nice boost, as we saw on the 24th of December when shares traded up about 5% over the next two days. It means the company is a step closer to bringing the full 400 MW online for CoreWeave and collecting that milestone-based revenue. Next to look out for are Buildings 1 and 2; each of these buildings is part of the remaining 300 MW of the contracted campus capacity for CoreWeave.

data.title: Docusign: A Cheap SaaS Growth Play
data.description:
SaaS company and e-Signature leader DocuSign (DOCU) reported better-than-expected earnings for its third fiscal quarter on Thursday after the bell, which nonetheless caused a ~7% drop in the company's share price amid concerns over slowing growth. DocuSign continued to mainta high gross margins in the third-quarter, however, and the software enterprise continues to benefit from a decent flow of enterprise customers to its Saas platform. I believe investors overreacted to DocuSign's revenue guidance, which came in below expectations, and I see rebound potential for DOCU given its strong execution in its core market for e-Signature solutions.

Previous rating

I rated shares of Docusign a speculative buy in my last work in June 2025 --Why I Am Buying The Q1 Crash Hand Over Fist -- because I liked the firm's adoption of AI in its core software business and DocuSign posted improving results, especially in terms of free cash flow. Shares of DocuSign dropped more than 7% after the company reported earnings on Thursday which may present investors with another buying opportunity.

DocuSign beat expectations by a solid margin

The software company beat analysts expectations on both the top and the bottom line for its fourth fiscal quarter: it reported adjusted earnings of $1.01 per-share which beat the average prediction by $0.09 per-share. The top line also beat expectations, by $10.9M, and came in at $818.4M.

DocuSign generated record revenue of $818M in its third fiscal quarter, showing 8% year-over-year growth. The market leader in e-Signature solutions also saw a small (1 PP) sequential revenue growth deceleration, however, which, together with a slightly disappointing near term revenue forecast, led to selling pressure on Thursday. DocuSign's subscription revenue, which makes up 98% of the software enterprise's consolidated revenues, grew slightly faster, at 9% Y/Y, leading to total revenues of $801M.

DocuSign is still heavily reliant on direct distribution channels to generate revenue growth and generates the majority of its sales from enterprise clients that are using the firm's Cloud-backed software suite. DocuSign is a leader in e-Signature solutions and offers companies tools to decentralize and streamline workflows.

While the company has originally focused on safe e-Signature processes in order to simplify agreement processes, its software product portfolio has expanded progressively over time and now includes Intelligent Agreement Management services, such as AI-assisted contract reviews, identity verifications, workflow automations etc.

DocuSign is a slow-growth, but high-margin business and the software enterprise has the profile to prove it. In the most recent quarter, DocuSign generated a gross margin of 81.8%, showing a small, negligible margin contraction of 0.2 PP Q/Q. For comparison, Adobe (ADBE), a key rival in the software SaaS market and a direct competitor for DocuSign in the e-Signature segment, had a gross margin of 89.3% (as of the August quarter).

Adobe's gross margins, on a TTM basis and in dollar terms, are about 8.4X higher than DocuSign's, and the margin differential is about 10 PP. This makes Adobe significantly more profitable and allows the company to retain a higher share of its earnings. Adobe competes directly with DocuSign with its own e-Signature product Acrobat Sign which is bundled with its widely-used Acrobat PDF suite.

Outlook for 2026 and my expectations for growth

DocuSign is seeing an expanding total addressable market as it is growing its Intelligent Agreement Management services. In addition, I see an opportunity for the company to pursue agentic AI opportunities more aggressively in the future. Agentic AI is a huge growth opportunity for SaaS companies in general, but especially for those that already have complimentary software products. Since DocuSign already works with workflow automation, as an example, I expect to see a more vigorous pursuit of AI-driven growth opportunities in 2026.

Agentic AI is already a big business, and set to get even bigger as companies and consumers are both more willing to adopt AI products and services. The Agentic AI market, according to Precedence Research, is set for a 26X factor increase over the next decade, with the market estimated to be worth ~$200B by FY 2034. One company that is exploring Agentic AI aggressively is SoundHound AI (SOUN)... which I covered here. Salesforce is another SaaS company that is making big moves and bets heavily on broad-scale AI product adoption across the enterprise landscape: Agentforce Now A Massive Catalyst. DocuSign is also a user of AI which is likely to be set to be the company's next growth frontier.

The pursuit of opportunities such as agentic AI -- AI contract agents that cost-efficiently review documents/contracts/proposals -- and workflow optimization could make DocuSign's IAM platform more appealing to enterprise customers and help it pivot away from the core e-Signature business which is seeing growing competition and slowing growth. If DocuSign pivots successfully to higher-value, higher-growth enterprise (IAM) services, shares may be subjected to a revaluation as well.

For the fourth quarter, DocuSign sees only 7% year-over-year revenue growth, highlighting core segment expansion challenges. The disappointing near term revenue guidance resulted in selling pressure after the firm's earnings report for Q3'26 which in my opinion creates an opportunity to buy DOCU, but only if you have confidence in the company's ability to pivot towards IAM services successfully.

DocuSign’s valuation vs. SaaS rivals

Shares of Docusign have fallen 15% in the last year amid concerns about slowing growth in the core e-Signature market. Concerns over slowing billings/revenue growth have therefore also led to a contraction in the company's earnings multiplier: DocuSign is currently valued at a forward price-to-earnings ratio of 17.6X which still makes shares 40% more expensive than those of key rival Adobe ... which has a P/E ratio of 12.6X. DocuSign, however, has recently seen its forward price-to-earnings ratio drop below its 5-year average P/E ratio of 19.1X which may signal that the company is currently undervalued, in a historical context.

Prospects for a higher valuation for DocuSign will depend on whether or not the company is able to pivot to higher-margin services in the enterprise market. The agentic AI market presents the company with a huge opportunity, as does AI in general. DocuSign is still expected to operate profitably in the future and grow its EPS going forward. In fact, the SaaS platform is actually expected to grow faster than Adobe: a 19.3% long term EPS growth rate for DOCU compares to a 14.3% growth rate for ABDE.

In my June work I calculated a fair value for DocuSign's shares of $103, based off of the firm's historical P/S ratio, which resulted in a buy rating. I maintain this price target for now, as well as my rating, as I have not seen a major deterioration or improvement of operating fundamentals for the SaaS company in the third-quarter. In the longer term, a revaluation to this price target will only be possible if investors see a gradual improvement in the company's revenue growth rate and a changing revenue mix.

Risks with DocuSign

OpenAI is integrating APIs into its Chat.GPT model that allow companies to sift through contracts and streamline workflows while Adobe has found success with its Adobe Sign product, a tool that helps customers utilize the strength of its document ecosystem. OpenAI clearly represents an emerging threat in the automated document processing space so if DocuSign fails to transition its business and pivot towards IAM services, the company's share price may continue to languish in 2026.

Final thoughts

The SaaS platform is seeing high single-digit revenue growth, has a strong subscription business and high, stable gross margins. The company beat top and bottom line estimates for Q3'26 by good margins. However, challenges remain. Growth is broadly slowing and the outlook for Q4'26 revenue growth was a disappointment as well.

I am sticking to my buy rating for now as I see potential for DocuSign to leverage its software platform to move into the agentic AI market more vigorously in 2026 and utilize AI as a lever for growth, especially when it comes to the use of AI agents to do contract reviews and workflow automations. Shares are trading below the 5-year average P/E ratio which may signal that DocuSign's revenue and earnings growth potential are undervalued.

data.title: 2026 Outlook: Pushing Limits
data.description:
Transcript

Oscar Pulido: 2025 was the year AI leapt from boardroom talking points to real-world line items, and in 2026, that momentum only looks set to accelerate.

Companies are pouring record levels of capital into the infrastructure that will power this technology. Governments are reevaluating policy through the lens of productivity gains and competitive positioning, and markets are shifting as the ripple effects of AI spread through every corner of the economy.

But beneath the headlines lies a deeper story. How this massive AI buildout intersects with corporate balance sheets, why traditional diversification may be losing some of its punch, and how investors can navigate a world where micro-dynamics are increasingly the macro picture.

Welcome to The Bid, where we break down what's happening in the markets and explore the forces changing the economy and finance. I'm Oscar Pulido. Today I'm pleased to welcome back Jean Boivin, head of the BlackRock Investment Institute, to help us unpack the 2026 global outlook.

We'll dig into the three big themes shaping BII’s view for the year ahead and how other mega forces are set to evolve in 2026, and where he sees opportunities developing across markets outside of AI, like emerging economies, Japan and Europe.

Jean, thank you so much for joining us on The Bid.

Jean Boivin: It's great to be here.

Oscar Pulido: Well, believe it or not, the last time we had you on, we were talking about the outlook for 2025, and now we're talking about the outlook for 2026. I know the BlackRock Investment Institute has recently met with all the investors from around BlackRock and got together to talk about the themes for next year. And in fact, there are three themes that the BII is talking about. What are those three themes that we should know?

Jean Boivin: So, it's great to be here, and let’s just put that a little bit into context. We've been talking for the last few years, and we'll talk more today about the fact that we're in an era of transformation, something we haven't been through for a very long time – decades - shaped by mega forces. But that has been creating an environment that has a couple of core features. Markets are driven by a very few forces at play, which makes them concentrated and leads to an environment where it's very difficult to avoid making big calls and there's no real place to hide or to be neutral.

Going into this year, that story's evolving, and we have three themes. The first one is that micro is macro. The sheer scale of what we're talking about in terms of AI buildout is making this decision, having macro implications. The second is leveraging up. So, there's no way we can go on this transformation without having more debt and leveraging up. And that's going to have implication about investment opportunities. And the third theme is around the possibility of a diversification mirage, the fact that we might be lured to diversification in some aspect where it's not really real, so things to unpack, but these are the three themes.

Oscar Pulido: And you mentioned mega forces. This is now a term that has been with us for a couple of years, and the BlackRock Investment Institute pioneered the thought of these big structural changes that are going on in the global economy that are going to have a big impact on markets and returns. While there were several mega forces that were talked about, it seems like a big focus of the 2026 outlook is centered on the mega force of artificial intelligence, AI. So, why is that? Why is it such a big focus for the outlook for the next year?

Jean Boivin: So, we had five mega forces. Mega means they're big, but it turns out that even within those mega forces, there's one that is dominating quite a bit now. And that's AI, as you say. Why is that? The scale of the CapEx, deployment that is already started and is running faster than we were expecting last year, and our outlook last year was putting out there pretty ambitious number. We went through them already for the first year. The scale is so big that it is already driving the macro.

So, we look at growth in the US in 2025, and we got three times the contribution of non-recession investment than typically is the case. So that's all AI. We were sitting at this time last year, and we were debating about policy uncertainty that could lead to depressed investment. That was a consensus view back then. And yet, 2025 has been the massive investment boom. And why? Because AI has been dominating everything else, all the traditional macro aspects.

So, the reason why this is so central to our theme is that it's driving everything. And in the outlook, you'll see, even though we have many topics we cover, on each of these topics, AI finds a way to bubble up.

Oscar Pulido: There are definitely a lot of mentions of AI as you read through the outlook, and you talk about CapEx, or capital expenditures, that are sizable in amount. Remind us of what some of those numbers are. What have they been? What do you think they're going to be next year? And what are the projections for the next couple of years?

Jean Boivin: So, we are looking at a range from $5tn to 8tn by 2030. Again, this is ambition. It's not realized yet. If that happens, if we do get to these numbers by 2030, that would make it the fastest capital buildout ever. Not like a recent memory, it's ever. So, we're talking about something that is totally unprecedented.

Oscar Pulido: So, you mentioned the $5-8 trillion in terms of CapEx buildout by 2030. I think there's a question though that most people are asking and that even the markets were asking. At that very end of 2025, particularly in November, we started to see a little bit of a pullback in AI stocks. They've done well over the course of the year, but they had a bit of a pause, and I think the reason is people are wondering, is AI in a bubble? How do you think about it when people ask that question?

Jean Boivin: Well, the first thing I'd say is, I don't think this is the right frame or the most useful frame for investors. Maybe we are in a bubble. Even when we are in a bubble, it takes a long time to know we're in one. And it's really only in hindsight that it's clear. But moreover, the reason why we're talking about this now is because we're talking about unprecedented numbers. So, these numbers I've just thrown out a minute ago are huge, and admittedly, you jump to, maybe it's too much, and maybe we're talking about bubble.

But the potential for revenues that AI will generate, the potential transformative implications of AI is also unprecedented. So, you need to look at both sides of the ledger, if you will, to make an assessment. And so rather than thinking about bubble, we think this is really about -- we have a clashing of orders of magnitude that are going on right now, and it's about trying to reconcile them and see whether they can add up. We think this is the frame that we need to carry now in the next few years is keep reassessing whether the spending we see, maps or not with the potential revenues.

The bottom line is that you can see how, at the macro level, these numbers of spending could justify themselves. You could see that happening, first by the tech existing business revenue line expanding, and I think there's room for that to happen, but eventually it's going to be about AI leading to new pools of revenue in many parts of the economy. It should be transforming everything we do. And once you start to put some numbers on this, you can see that at a macro level, eventually, it could add up.

There are two conditions for that to happen. And the first is we're going to need to see a breakout of growth from 2%. And I want to pause here because that's a big deal. But the reality is, if you look at the last 150 years in the US, the US has been at the frontier of innovation. And all of the innovation we've seen in the US - think of industrial revolution, electricity, internet, medical breakthroughs - all of this human ingenuity has just been enough to keep us on this 2% trend. We never broke out of it. So, saying that now we're going to be breaking out of it is a big statement to make.

So, I don't think we should take that casually. However, we do think it's conceivable for the first time because AI is something that is very special. The potential here is to accelerate the pace of innovation, so AI will help us innovate. And if that's the case, you could see how that can lead to a faster breakthrough in science, and this is how you could get acceleration of innovation that leads to a breakout from 2%. So not a given, we'll need to track it. But for the first time, I think you can tell a conceivable story for why it would happen. So that's the first piece.

Oscar Pulido: You mentioned the 2% figure, and I just want to be clear. That's GDP growth in the US for the last 150 years has trended around 2%. And what you're saying is that the AI revolution, the AI mega force, could cause that number to accelerate?

Jean Boivin: That’s exactly right. But the second part that needs to happen is the fact that even if in the macro level, we do generate all these revenues, it's not clear who's going to be able to extract or capture these revenues yet. So, now the tech industry will be able to capture for some time, but as it gets embedded in everything, it's going to be across sectors, and it's going to be about competition for revenues across sectors.

Who wins is unclear, and that's why it's going to become a real alpha active story. So at the end of the day, we are bullish on AI still because even though these ambitions are big, we're just starting. And we're about an eighth to a fifth of the scale eventually. We don't think that these companies or the build-out will be on autopilot. So as things play out, ambitions will be revised. And we'll need to track this, but it's way too soon in our view to conclude that it's overdone. So, we're bullish on AI, which leads us to be bullish on risk more broadly, and that's how we go into 2026.

Oscar Pulido: Right. At its core, the concern about a bubble is that there's a lot of money being spent, and will it generate a return on investment, particularly for the companies that are outlaying these big capital expenditures? And perhaps that's why you're saying the micro impacts the macro. That's the first theme of the 2026 outlook.

So, let's talk about the second theme, which you mentioned is leveraging up, which actually is also related to AI and talks about this observation that big tech firms, essentially these companies that are spending on capital expenditures towards AI, are starting to issue debt as a way to finance these big capital expenditures. So talk about why is that important? And what is the implication for all this corporate debt being issued in an economy?

Jean Boivin: So, we think this is something that is profoundly changing the nature of the market environment. We're talking about a phenomenon where we are spending, investing and building before we get the benefit and the revenues that will come down the road. So, there's a gap in timing between when you need to spend and the revenue will come. By nature, implies that we're going to have to bridge that, and the way to bridge that is to get more in debt or to leverage up. So that's what the real heart of it is, financing hump - and it's not good or bad. It's like, you buy a house, you don't have all the revenues immediately that will justify your investment, but over time, you'll get it. And the only way to do it is to leverage up personally. So that's exactly the same analogy here.

We're going to have to go through this phase, neither good nor bad. And that's in the context where government balance sheets are already leveraged up quite a bit. The system will be probably more sensitive to shocks that's going to come as a result. It's going to be a feature of this environment; we'll need to navigate that. It also means that because of the interplay between the government debt and now the leveraging up of the private sector, big moves in government debt yields. Big moves in interest rate could create more disruptions in the private sector as they leverage up.

So, that's the nature of where we're going. But it's important also to realize that we are starting from a corporate debt side that is extremely healthy, very healthy. So, it's not a red flag. Again, it's not good or bad. I think there's room to move in that direction. But that also means a different kind of sensitivity of the markets, which investor will need to be take into account.

In terms of investment opportunities, this theme means that we're going to be tapping more heavily in the source of financing as we leverage up, and the government will not be there to finance those as it might have been in another transformations. As a result, these diversification of sources of investment in financing I think will be a structural tailwind that will continue to expand the role of private credit as a source of funding for these projects. And for investors, as a result creates ongoing opportunities. So that's one of the key theme implications. And then the other would be around government debt in a leverage up environment. We're going to see, I think, pressure on yields to continue to go up. And this AI theme's going to be over the medium term inflationary. So that leads us to be a bit more careful on long-term yields and underweight long-term US treasuries in particular.

Oscar Pulido: We've talked about equity markets, and now we've talked about fixed income markets. And the commonality has been that it's AI related, right? We're talking about how the build-out of AI impacts both asset classes, which makes me think when AI is such an encompassing theme in markets, at some point, you do want to think about diversification. And where do you think about diversification in this sort of market regime that we're in now?

Jean Boivin: Yeah, so that's our diversification mirage theme here. And the point is that it's going to be -- it is very difficult to find diversification because it's the nature of this environment that transformation, a couple of mega forces that are driving these markets. And, as I said, you're either with or not with these force, or you're with AI or you're not convinced and you're not -- but you can't really be in a halfway house or neutral.

So that makes diversification very difficult, and it also creates this lure. It might be appealing. You might think you're doing diversification, but it's an active call against AI. It might be the right call, but if you do it, it should be with conviction. So, this is the mirage, right? So, there's something that is an active call but is being positioned as being something of a diversification.

Same thing we've seen earlier this year around the fact that US policy uncertainty was creating some concern by global investors and there was an argument to say, well, maybe we should diversify away from the US, have more in Europe and so on, might be the right thing to do, but that's not diversification. It's an active call. Taking a stance on what in the US versus Europe. So, point is, a lot of what used to be seen as diversification properly in the past might not be true diversification in this environment.

Instead, we need to think about more creatively about diversification. So instead, we think there are strategies like market-neutral strategies that are really like trying to be insulated from the broad market movements and try to generate alpha around this. It's not the traditional diversification, where you want to think about allocating in this environment, not to be fully exposed to the broad market movement.

Another aspect is thematic that are likely to work in multiple scenarios. So, we think infrastructure is something that works across many of the mega forces and across many ways in which they might pan out. So, a thematic like this is probably more diversified in the sense that it works across more portfolios.

And finally, this is stretching the idea of diversification, but fundamentally, it's a world where we won't be able to diversify as much as we used to. We need to get on with the program. And one real way to manage this is going to have to require to be thinking about more plan B and be ready to pivot. So that's not typical diversification, but that's an implication of this environment where with less passive diversifier, we're going to need to more actively pivot and think about a plan B if the AI story becomes more challenge for instance.

Oscar Pulido: And Jean, let me come back to another theme that you talked about earlier. You were mentioning private credit and how tech companies are tapping the capital markets in order to fund their capital spending, and private credit is one of those ways. I actually think about one of the five mega forces, while we've been talking about AI, is the future of finance and that is private credit, but I think it also includes a few other themes within it that might play out in 2026. Maybe talk a little bit about what your views are there.

Jean Boivin: So, I think, beyond the private credit aspect in the future of finance, we're thinking about the reshaping of the financial architecture that is driven by financial innovation -- digital financial innovation. And you can think about tokenization as a big part of it. The crypto landscape and clearly stablecoins is at the core of this. It's been a big story in 2025. It's infancy in many ways, but it's on a very steep adoption curve. The adoption is widening, and now we're seeing that being integrated in mainstream payment system. This has been accelerated by policy and regulation in the US this year, and we expect that to continue to broaden. We're going to see broadening of the access to the dollar as a result of international adoption of stablecoin that are backed by US dollar assets.

And that is the beginning. We think there's going to be questions around how the financial system reorganized with itself around this. And that is still to be determined because these will make payment more accessible globally, but they will also mean that you can maybe store your savings short-term in a stablecoin or have a portfolio between your bank account and your portfolio. So, there's going to be a competition that will appear with this.

Banks might be starting to issue their own stablecoin, so that's a pretty energetic space that is building out right now. Big theme. It's in its infancy, but with a lot of potential. It's not necessarily an investment story in stablecoin, but it's thinking about how does it shake up the financial system, and as a result, maybe, for instance, we think in European financials. All this digitalization in AI creates additional expected return for the sector. So, that's going to be about tracing out these impacts across asset classes.

Oscar Pulido: That's definitely a new component to the global outlook that we haven't talked about in years past but speaks to why these are real structural changes that are going on in the economy.

Jean, we talked about micro. It's now macro. Talked about leveraging up. We talked about diversification and the mirage of diversification. What are some other themes that investors should be considering over the next six to 12 months that maybe we haven't talked about thus far?

Jean Boivin: Yeah, so we talked about, like, well, I think a lot about AI. But if you look more broadly, there's more, right? Japan's equities continue to be attractive to us. It's been a story over the last couple of years. We see that continuing. There's corporate reforms still having momentum. We're seeing growth catch up. And ultimately, even though the BOJ needs continuing attention, might be sounding hawkish like they do or they've done the recent past. Ultimately, I don't think inflation is a real problem in Japan. And so, we're going to see more fiscal support - inflation that is benign. So, a pretty constructive environment. So, Japan, we like.

Europe - we need to make a nuance here that is actually very important. In terms of broad market exposure, we prefer the US. But in Europe, we put out a paper recently on Europe and the investment renaissance that Europe might be eventually embarking on. Right now, it's more selective. So we see sectors like finance, as I mentioned, healthcare, defense. These are pockets where we think there is being overweight. Allocation makes sense. And great opportunities for investors already now. And then there's the potential as Europe builds out more of the defense side. We see more fiscal support coming. And if we move towards signs of greater capital market deepening, which is really the big thing there, if it were to happen, it would be a game changer and would turn this selective story into a broader story. So, this is not yet on the table, but it's something to watch and could become more real over the course of 2026.

In EM, this is a story of 2025. We had massive return in EM in 2025. Made possible by a weakening US dollar, clearly part of it this year. We continue to see a stable dollar or mostly neutral but constructive for EM. Yields in the US that are still fairly low, and as a result, help to a favorable backdrop to EM. And their fiscal position overall is productive. So, EM is attractive, but we see that more on the hard currency debt side. So, these will be like the three kind of other areas I would mention on top of everything else we talked about.

Oscar Pulido: Jean, we mentioned we haven't seen you since last year when you talked about the 2025 outlook, but we’ve spent a lot of time with your colleagues at the BlackRock Investment Institute, who have guided us through geopolitical events, through the tariffs that were announced back in April, and through some of the macro events that have happened throughout the course of the year. And then, great to have you back as we look ahead to 2026. Thank you for giving us that guidance, and thank you for doing it here on The Bid.

Jean Boivin: Thanks, Oscar. It's a pleasure to be here.

data.title: Netflix: Warner Bros Acquisition Is A Bold Step Towards A Trillion Dollar Future
data.description:
December 5th, 2025, will be remembered as a pivotal day for the media industry, as Warner Bros (WBD), owner of arguably one of the most valuable studios and IP libraries, joins forces with what is undeniably the most valuable streaming platform, Netflix (NFLX).

For Netflix, the $72 billion deal is highly likely to set forth a challenging period, which will include intense regulatory scrutiny, consistent pressure from industry participants, and lots of uncertainties regarding leverage, cost synergies, and integration plans.

Despite that, it's hard to envision a scenario where Netflix doesn't become a far more valuable business with Warner Bros assets under its wing.

Let's dive in.

Netflix Buying Warner Bros - Deal Terms, Valuation & Timeline

Netflix and Warner Bros entered into a definitive agreement under which Netflix will acquire Warner's film and television studios, HBO Max, and HBO.

Netflix will pay $27.75 per share of WBD, after the latter spins off the rest of its businesses, representing an $82.7 billion enterprise value for the streaming and studio assets, and $72 billion in equity value.

The transaction is expected to be completed in Q3-26, the target date for the spinoff.

Netflix reportedly plans to take on $59 billion in debt to finance the acquisition, on top of cash and stock.

Dissecting Clues For Netflix's Plans

The first thing that's critical to understand, Netflix's executives are not going to say anything that can hurt the deal, plain and simple. For instance, they won't say they plan to shut off the HBO Max service, stop showing movies in theaters, lay off thousands of employees, cut content spend, stop licensing content to others, or raise prices for consumers when HBO content is added.

Therefore, we should try to search for clues to understand their actual plans.

In my mind, Netflix will, gradually, sunset HBO standalone. It will start by making it available exclusively as a bundled offering, whereby HBO streaming subscribers will automatically join Netflix for a certain period. Then, over time, it will simply become another "cube" in the service.

Note that Greg Peters said they will combine Warner Bros. entertainment with Netflix's reach and business model. Netflix was always proud of the simplicity of its offering and viewed it as an advantage against Disney (DIS) and others. I expected they will stick to that.

Netflix said it plans to maintain Warner's current operations and build on its strengths, which reads to me as cutting their weaknesses.

And, as always, when companies say they will add value to consumers, it likely means they will be able to raise prices accordingly.

The Bullish Scenario

In my mind, the bullish scenario includes a best-in-class library and content machine, driving significant growth across members, ARPU, and ads, while returning to operational leverage.

Unparalleled Content Library & Future Content Funnel

One of the biggest arguments against Netflix over the years was that its content is inferior. Under the bullish scenario, this argument goes out the door.

Under the bullish scenario, Warner Bros assets will be fully integrated into Netflix. This includes the extraordinary existing library, which includes titles like Big Bang Theory, The Sopranos, Game of Thrones, Wizard of Oz, DC Universe, Friends, Harry Potter, and so many more. It will also include future content from HBO, which is widely viewed as the maker of the highest quality of shows.

Translating Into More Subscribers, Higher ARPU, Higher Engagement & Ad Revenue

Netflix is cheaper than some of its peers in certain plans, specifically ad-supported plans, despite being the most-watched platform. Adding HBO will immediately add to Netflix's share of viewing, but I believe this is a scenario where the sum is larger than its parts. HBO shows on the Netflix platform are likely to generate orders of magnitude more engagement than they do today, which means Netflix will likely surpass 10% share of viewing.

This will then allow Netflix to a) gain more members; b) increase prices c) generate more ad revenue, which is correlated to engagement.

In Year Three, Netflix Will Go Back To Its Steady Margin Expansion Path

The reason Netflix has delivered such attractive returns since its 2022 bottom is that it has proven the operational leverage potential of its business model.

While the first few years of integration are likely to be challenging and bumpy, I expect the same operational leverage trajectory will emerge on the other side.

The Bearish Scenario

The bearish scenario includes two options. Option one, the deal goes through but Netflix fails to execute on the bullish scenario. This will likely mean Netflix's stock become a significant underperformer. Option two, the deal doesn't go through, and Netflix's reputation among regulators and talent suffers. This option isn't as problematic, because at the end of the day, I don't think talent will punish Netflix for trying, and consumers are probably very happy about this acquisition anyways.

Valuation & What Should Investors Do Now

At the time of writing, Netflix shares are falling about 5% in pre-market trading, with shares hovering around $98. This adds to the 23% drawdown the stock has been experiencing since June, part of which occurred as Netflix's buying Warner Bros has become more probable.

To me, this is a reasonable reaction given the extreme level of risk that Netflix is taking upon itself, but it's obviously pricing the deal at a negative value.

Let's start with Netflix standalone. At $99 per share, Netflix is trading at one of its lowest valuation multiples since the 2022 fallout, around a 31x P/E. If it weren't for the deal, I'd argue that this is an attractive valuation for Netflix, which was on track to drive 20%+ EPS growth for many years.

Transitioning to the deal itself. An $82 billion enterprise value represents a 4x multiple on run-rate sales for Warner Bros Studios & Streaming segment, and a 20x multiple on its run-rate EBITDA. By itself, not too terrible.

In addition, Netflix estimates $2-$3 billion in savings over the first three years, simply by cutting duplicate SG&A and technology spend. This doesn't take into account many future synergies, and the type of stuff I referred to as things Netflix won't say before they get all the necessary approvals.

To me, at the current valuation, Netflix investors get the entire optionality value of the deal for free, but given the inherent risks, I wouldn't buy aggressively here. If the stock drops to the $90 range, that's where I'll get more greedy.

Conclusion

Netflix's acquisition of Warner Bros. is unquestionably increasing the long-term opportunity for Netflix investors and likely accelerates its path to becoming a $1 trillion company.

Because Netflix can't really detail its long-term plans for the Warner Bros. assets, and because it's set to face intense scrutiny over the coming quarters, the best thing to do, in my mind, is hold tight.

After the deal gets done, targeting Q3-26, things will get much clearer. Until then, it's going to be a bumpy ride, but I argue that it's going to be more than worth it.

data.title: The Fed Is Mending
data.description:
The Federal Reserve trimmed U.S. interest rates by a quarter point recently. However, it seems quite reluctant to cut again in December, in my opinion. The Fed has stated that it will halt its quantitative tightening program amid signs of stress in our money markets.

The Federal Reserve, at their last meeting, did cut U.S. interest rates but warned a further reduction this year was not a “foregone conclusion,” as tariffs and our current government shutdown make the outlook murky. They also expressed concerns about America’s weak job growth, which prompted the Fed’s move to halt efforts to shrink its balance sheet starting in December.

“A further reduction of the policy rate at the December meeting is not a foregone conclusion,” Fed Chair Jay Powell said. “I always say that it’s a fact that we don’t make decisions in advance. But I’m saying something in addition here: that it’s not to be seen as a foregone conclusion, in fact far from it.”

Markets had bet heavily on the prospect of another quarter-point cut this year, pricing in a large chance of another move in December ahead of Powell’s remarks. The odds of a cut fell after his comments, in my opinion. Powell said the dearth of adequate data caused by the shutdown would also be a factor in the FOMC’s thinking. “What do you do when you’re driving in the fog? You slow down,” Powell said, adding there was “a possibility” this would influence the debate on December 10.

Powell further said that an array of forecasts and approaches to risk among policymakers “were reflected in strongly differing views in today’s meeting.” Alongside the rate move, the FOMC announced it was pulling back from its quantitative tightening program amid concerns that short-term funding for some banks was becoming too tight. The New York Fed would begin reinvesting all the proceeds of maturing Treasury debt held by the central bank into the government debt market from December 1, the committee said. The Fed will also reinvest $35bn a month from maturing mortgage-backed securities into the Treasuries market from December 1.

“Conditions in the labor market seem to be gradually cooling,” Powell said, though he later added this was mostly down to Trump’s immigration crackdown. “There’s not a supply of workers showing up for jobs,” he stated. The recent reductions in expectations also comes after Trump’s campaign to push the Fed to dramatically lower borrowing costs.

The Fed bought trillions of dollars of Treasuries after the Covid-19 pandemic, increasing its balance sheet to $9tn and flooding the financial system with cash. Since 2022, the central bank has pulled back, allowing Treasuries and government mortgage-backed securities to mature without buying replacements.

In my view, the doubt has now been cast, and the markets are reacting accordingly. My amount of caution has now increased, with Chairman Powell making clear that the Fed is in no hurry to follow President Trump’s request. We have two branches of the government that are clearly not in sync, and this is a cause of some serious consternation and fear. I would not be rushing into either the bond or stock market now and investing without some very diligent thinking.

The writing may be on the wall, but the meaning is just not discernable.

Original Source: Author
