data.title: Volvo: No Real Upside, Even After Recent Delivery Numbers
data.description:
I haven't made a secret about my relatively neutral or slightly negative view on Volvo (OTCPK:VOLAF). For the past few quarters. Most of my articles have been negative or neutral on the company, and I haven't been keen to add. This hasn't, looking at returns, turned out all that well for investors either. Since the latest article, the company's returns are negative 3.35%, relative to a 6.65% upside, close to a 10% difference, which is beyond the realm of a rounding error or aberration in my world.

As you can see, this wasn't the first article where I was "correct" on Volvo, insofar as VOLAF or similar ADR's go. The market, including the native Swedish one, seems to have an unfortunate tendency to overvalue Volvo at times. I'm writing this update on Volvo AB (not Volvo Cars, that's a different company, and one I consider to be even more problematic, but for a whole host of other reasons), for a few reasons. The company recently reported 3Q, and also very recently came in with delivery numbers.

Volvo isn't in any way a bad business. The company fundamentals, as I have repeatedly pointed out in previous articles, are all very solid. The company is A-rated, and its current dividend, although subject to change, is over 6% for the native ticker. The company has a strong trend to beat its estimates, but overall manages a relatively medium EPS growth rate of about 7.5% per year for the past 20 years. Not bad in any way.

However, during downturns, the company's results can go truly bad. During the GFC in -09, the company saw negative EPS, and it has seen negative EPS trends several times for the past 20 years. Current estimates for 2025E also come to a 26% AEPS drop, before recovering. But the bottom line is, we won't see the 2024A results, if estimates are to be believed, even in 2027E.

So what have recent results and delivery numbers given us to look at here?

Volvo Upside and fundamentals - seeing what the next few years might bring

Like many companies I look at, or like many things in my career, the appeal of this company comes down to how you view its potential. Essentially, the question is how you view a company that is expected to, in terms of AEPS, go nowhere from 2024A to 2027E - because that's what the forecast is currently saying. The 2042A results were AEPS of 24.7 SEK, and the 2027E results are forecasted at 24 SEK - below the 2024 results.

Volvo has been on a very long journey of improvement. It's been more than a decade now, but these results have seen improvements in expense management, productivity, cost efficiencies, and "cutting the fat" from the portfolio in ways that peers seem to be unable to do. Even if what the company does is technically something that peers would be able to replicate, doing so would take effort and time.

Another big advantage that speaks in favor of Volvo is that over time, the company has been able to grow its service revenue very quickly. In terms of the share of service in the sales mix, the company is now seeing one of the highest shares of service sales in its peer group. That peer group, by the way, includes PACCAR (PCAR) as well as Caterpillar (CAT). Most of the peers are overvalued. As I said about Volvo, it depends on how you look at it. In Europe, it's fair to say that Volvo compares with Daimler (OTCPK:DTRUY) and Traton SE, both of which I believe are fundamentally worse than Volvo. I have, however, invested in Daimler, because it has been more valuation-appealing.

On a high-level basis, the trucking market is currently seeing a cyclical shift. The reason for this cyclical shift is still related to things like COVID-19, regulatory uncertainty, and weak consumer confidence. Demand weakness is expected to continue to be weak at the group, for at least another 1-2 years. I do not foresee an increase to pre-COVID-19 margin levels for the foreseeable future, although I see the potential for a recovery in sales in NA.

A few risks that are significant to the company, even after a good 3Q25, are as follows.

Daimler is probably one of my favorite companies in this sector - it's why I invest in it more than Volvo. It has to do with the valuation that the company often gets to, below €33/share, which makes it almost cheap. Furthermore, if Daimler can execute a fundamental turnaround of its operations, then it can shoehorn in on some of Volvo's "turf" here. Also, moving away from Europe and into China, I don't see that the current valuation takes properly into account the very real risk of Chinese companies and their EV technology - though any threat to a market like this one is to be taken with a bit of salt (we're talking decades of entrenched market leadership).

Still, price is a compelling argument, and that's where the Chinese brands can compete.

Beyond that, some 3Q25 results - not great. The company's margins, as I've said, continue to drop, and I don't see them recovering to the previous levels.

Both deliveries and machine deliveries are down, despite progress in the electrification.

EV was really one of the few sales segments that saw solid numbers, with fully electric vehicle orders down 4%, but deliveries up 25%. Still, it should be considered that these growth numbers in deliveries are still in a very early stage. So for it to be slowing down as you see here (though it's part of a quarterly pattern), we should see what happens in the European home markets.

What can be said is that vehicle sales are down - this also includes the quarterly pattern trends.

As we can see, buses, equipment, and Penta numbers are good for the year, but that's about it. Service sales are down, too, and this was one of the company's fundamental arguments. On the product side, the company has scored very good marks on its new products, such as Renault Trucks and Volvo Trucks, but product excellence has never been Volvo's issue. They have always made good trucks and good products.

We need to remember that this is a very volatile market. But unlike with Daimler, the stock seems to be less volatile in the short term in response to these patterns.

I don't view 3Q25 as a great quarter - nor did the market. it dropped to almost attractive levels when they were released, only to bounce back up with a 3.18% growth today (Thursday, the 4th of December).

So what has happened today? Well, mostly a market surge in Europe. I'm up significantly in my portfolio today, but I don't see any specific reasons as to why today should be a surge - nor do I see a valuation-based surge or upside justification here. In fact, for many of its segments, the company's market share has actually been dropping. Volvo's market share in BEV has dropped in Europe, same with NA, with YTD based on August. Brazil is flat - the only part that's significantly increasing is Renault Trucks.

I'm not saying that Volvo is a poor investment - it's not. I'm just saying I want a better price before I were to go into this investment here. I believe the market is underestimating headwinds coming in the next few quarters, not only tariffs, but things like macro. Just tariffs are expected to be about or around ~100 bps of margin impact, and for a company like this, that is non-trivial. There are many moving parts where the visibility, or lack of it, doesn't really justify the exuberance that is implied by a 280+ SEK native share price.

For that reason, my view on the company's valuation is now as follows.

Volvo Valuation - A small moat is there, but it's currently too opaque from tariffs

The dividend doesn't save this company, or constitute by itself an investment reason, not when I believe that looking at this particular business and its likely forecast. What I mean is that the company is currently traded close to 15x, and its P/E average over time is significantly below this. Even with earnings reversal, Volvo seems unlikely here to generate market-beating returns, less than 8% per year, and of that, over 6% is the dividend yield. So if that dividend goes lower, and as you can see, the forecast is for the next year to bring a significant dividend cut, then this company's upside is going to be even less.

Note that without the dividends, the company's returns here are negative. This should convey to you what sort of potential the company has.

I would apply, as before, a 5-year average fair value target, coming to about 280 SEK/share. But that's the fair value target, not the PT. In my last article, I gave the company a PT of 255 SEK/share. I would maintain my PT here, though I also say there's potential for this to be lowered if the company's results are worse than expected.

2025E is expected to be a significant decline from 2024's top-earnings levels. I do not see a reason why the company, based on tariffs and macro, should expect to outperform at a level that would justify this price, or a higher price, and deliver a significant upside.

Analyst targets are slightly more exuberant than mine. Current average targets are at 288 SEK/share, with a 242 SEK low end and 350 SEK high-end target, a fair bit of variance to the price target/s for the company. However, the ratings do not reflect the average PT. Only 5 analysts are at "BUY" here, with 9 at "HOLD", and 1 at "Underperform", 1 at a straight "SELL" rating. So in numbers, this is a "BUY", but in S&P Global ratings, they closely reflect my view on the company.

Alternatives to Volvo?

Daimler, I'd say - but not now.

I would forecast the company not at the 5-year average, but at the 2-3 year average, to better reflect the above-mentioned volatility, which obviously also affects Daimler. Based on an 8.8x P/E upside, returns here are below 15%, which is not good enough for me to invest in.

I view the sector and the companies in the sector as overvalued here. This includes, but is not limited to, Volvo AB.

Risks are as follows.

Risks & Considerations

The risk to my neutral thesis is considerable outperformance on the part of the company, which certainly could lead to a better rate of return, invalidating my rating and beating the market. However, as this would require significant outperformance on the company's end-markets that Volvo does not control, I view the likelihood of the company actually managing this as rather remote.

Based on this, I maintain the ADR target of $27/share for VLVLY.

Thesis

  • Volvo is one of the world-leading companies in the trucking field, CE, buses, and other machine and industrial applications. It has an excellent credit rating, a good upside at a conservative valuation, and owns some of the more appealing brands of trucks and CE in NA and EU. Based on this, when the price is good, this company becomes a "Buy" - but that is not the case as of 3Q25.
  • The company has an upside only if you allow for it to trade above a valuation based on historical averages that have not held for an extended period of time. I maintain my conservative PT of 255 SEK/share, seeing an upside to the upper trading levels if you manage to buy at that time.
  • However, as of 280+ SEK/share in December, which is what we have now for the native, I say that Volvo is a "Hold" and would continue being neutral here.

Here are my criteria and how the company fulfills them (italicized).

  • This company is overall qualitative.
  • This company is fundamentally safe/conservative, and well-run.
  • This company pays a well-covered dividend.
  • This company is currently cheap.
  • This company has a realistic upside that is high enough, based on earnings growth or multiple expansion/reversion.

The company fulfills 3 out of 3 quality indicators but 0 out of 2 valuation indicators. Based on this and a valuation target of $27/share where I see a risk/reward-adjusted upside that's attractive enough for me, I give the company a "Hold" rating as of December 2025.

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: Kennametal Remains A Top-Cut Prospect
data.description:
The last few months have been simply fantastic for shareholders of Kennametal (KMT). This is already a fascinating company from an operational standpoint, with the entire business built around it offering certain general engineering, transportation, earthworks, energy, aerospace, and defense materials technologies. Through its Metal Cutting segment, the company develops and sells high-performance tooling and metal-cutting products, as well as various services and technologies dedicated to the airframes, trucks, and other automobiles, ships, and other categories. And then, through its Infrastructure segment, it engages in the production of engineered tungsten carbide and ceramic components, earth-cutting tools, and some metallurgical powders that are used in the production of earth cutting tools, abrasive water jet nozzles, drilling and road milling offerings, and the like.

As you can tell, this is the kind of company that the economy relies on. When investments are being made into growth for the economy, this is the kind of firm that should theoretically do well. Recently, we have seen some gradual growth, even though broader economic conditions have become questionable. And as a result, shares of the business are now up 33.2% since I reaffirmed it as a "Buy" candidate back in August of this year. By comparison, the S&P 500 is up only 5%. Despite this surge in share price, the stock looks incredibly cheap, both on an absolute basis and relative to similar firms. And with management expecting further top-line and bottom-line growth this year, I believe that maintaining my bullish rating makes sense here.

Kennametal remains compelling

The only new data that we currently have regarding Kennametal covers through the first quarter of the company's 2026 fiscal year. During that time, revenue for the business came in at $498 million. That is up from the $481.9 million the business reported a year earlier. As revenue improved, profitability also increased. Net income, for instance, inched up from $22.1 million to $23.3 million. And on an adjusted basis, we saw a more robust increase from $22.6 million to $26.3 million. We saw operating cash flow plummet from $45.7 million to $17.5 million. If we adjust for changes in working capital, however, this metric also improved, growing from $61.1 million to $70.3 million. And finally, EBITDA for the business popped from $69.3 million to $74.3 million.

This overall trend is great to see, but a lot of context is needed. To get that context, we need to dig deeper. Perhaps the best place to start would be to dig into its Metal Cutting segment, which is the larger of the two that it has. Year over year, revenue for this unit grew from $296.9 million to $310.6 million. This rise was rather solid and was driven largely by a 3% increase in organic sales. It is worth noting that the rest of the increase can be chalked up to foreign currency fluctuations that worked in the firm's favor.

One thing that I find to be exciting about this business is that management does provide some pretty good detail regarding the end markets this segment services. As the image above illustrates, the big driver here ended up being its aerospace and defense markets. Overall revenue skyrocketed 18%, with a constant currency growth of 16%. This is a monumental amount of upside, but it's honestly not surprising. In the Americas, the business benefited from robust demand for its services.

Some of this is almost certainly strategic, as management emphasizes a greater presence in this part of the world. But I would be derelict in my duties if I did not mention that the aerospace market has been incredibly strong as of late anyway. In another article that I wrote about Boeing (BA), I detailed just how strong this industry currently is. Even though there was a lot of pain caused by the COVID-19 pandemic, air travel has since exploded, and it's likely that, over the next couple of decades at least, we will continue to see strong results here. Most obviously, Kennametal will be a beneficiary of this. And then, obviously, increased military spending in the United States also bodes well for a business of Kennametal’s nature. But strong industry growth is not as strong as what Kennametal has experienced here. By management's own admission, locking down additional customer contracts helped to boost its overall share of this end market, which is fantastic to see.

Another major area for Kennametal’s Metal Cutting segment during this time was the energy category. Although the Asia Pacific region helped here, the Americas once again proved a major source of expansion for the business. In some ways, this is the more exciting space, and the expansion that the firm saw here for this operating segment of 15% (12% on a constant currency basis) seems to have been tied at least in large part to increased power demand in the data center industry. In one article recently, I talked about how this space is expected to continue growing for the next several years. Management is most certainly a beneficiary of this trend. And, by extension, shareholders are too.

In fact, in the recent investor presentation that management provided, they made available for investors an entire slide dedicated to surging power demand coming from AI that represents a $250 million total addressable market opportunity for the business. This specifically involves precision-machined power generation engines and is expected to grow at an annualized rate of 10% for the 2023 through 2028 fiscal years. Given the firm's know-how and provision of advanced tooling technologies, it believes that it is a shoe-in when it comes to providing innovative tooling for next-generation engines, as well as certain materials needed for these tooling technologies. It already has a history of success in manufacturing complex engine components that are used in the production of high-horsepower gas and diesel engines as well. And with the company able to target not only OEMs, but also what it calls "tiered suppliers" and machine tool builders, it is in a solid position for even more growth as time goes on.

Even though it wasn't as exciting as the growth that we saw under the Metal Cutting segment, the firm’s Infrastructure segment also did well. Revenue grew from $185 million to $187.3 million year over year. This was even though the business took a 3% hit because of an asset divestiture. This was offset, fortunately, by a 3% organic increase in revenue, as the aerospace and defense markets surged 31% (28% on a constant currency basis). Strong industry demand certainly contributed here. However, management also acknowledged that project timing and the execution of its growth initiatives helped here.

Overall, it seems to me as though things are going quite well. And if the picture continues to come in strong, we should anticipate revenue this year of between $2.10 billion and $2.17 billion. That compares favorably to the $1.97 billion the company reported for the 2025 fiscal year. Unfortunately, this will largely be the result of higher prices and tariff surcharge realization that the company implements. That should add about 7% to the top line. Foreign currency is expected to add another 2%. Meanwhile, volume could be down by as much as 1% or up by as much as 3%, pointing toward a small increase. But still, growth is growth all the same and should be applauded. This is especially true when it involves higher profitability as well.

At the midpoint of guidance for adjusted earnings per share, the company should generate adjusted net profits of $115.2 million. That's up from the $104.7 million reported for 2025. Based on the data provided, it's likely that adjusted operating cash flow will come in at around $243.8 million. That would be a bit below the $247.5 million the company reported for last year. However, I do believe that EBITDA will be somewhere around $317.8 million. By comparison, in 2025, it was $293.1 million.

The higher revenue certainly helps here. But it's important to note that management is also working to cut costs. In fact, this year, the firm expects $35 million in restructuring savings. It's also planning to fully offset the impact that tariffs have had on it, and it is doing this while simultaneously continuing to push for growth opportunities. In fact, Kennametal's, unless nothing changes, capital expenditures this year should be somewhere around $90 million. The good news is that this is unlikely to stop the company from buying back stock as well. $15 million of dividends were also paid out during the most recent quarter alone, which signals management's interest in returning capital to shareholders. But that's not all on that front. During the first quarter of 2026, the company repurchased 475,000 shares in exchange for about $10 million. This is part of an ambitious $200 million share buyback program spread over three years, with about $70 million already being allocated toward the purchase of 3 million units.

Normally, I prefer that management teams focus on growth opportunities. But in cases where shares are cheap, I don't mind some share buybacks. Sometimes, I even favor them. And indeed, shares of Kennametal are certainly cheap. Using the estimates I provided already for 2026, as well as historical results for 2025, we can see in the chart above how the stock is currently priced. The price-to-earnings multiple is not particularly appealing. But when it comes to the cash flow multiples, things look quite nice. In addition to being cheap on an absolute basis, the stock is also cheap compared to other similar firms. In the table below, I stacked our candidate up against five similar enterprises. And what I found was that, across the board, it ended up being the cheapest of the group.

Company Price / Earnings Price / Operating Cash Flow EV / EBITDA
Kennametal 18.5 7.8 7.4
Tennant Company (TNC) 24.6 15.3 9.1
Helios Technologies (HLIO) 51.4 14.9 14.7
Hillenbrand (HI) 52.0 39.8 10.1
Albany International Corp (AIN) 26.4 8.8 26.7
Hillman Solutions Corp (HLMN) 44.8 14.2 9.5

Takeaway

Based on all the data currently available, I would say that things are going quite well for shareholders right now. Management is continuing to capture growth opportunities while cutting costs and returning capital to shareholders. This is the trifecta as far as most investors are concerned. When you add on top of this how the stock is priced, both on an absolute basis and relative to similar firms, I would argue that further upside is likely from here. That's why I don't have an issue keeping Kennametal rated a "Buy" right now.

data.title: Chipotle's Customers Are Revolting (Rating Downgrade)
data.description:
Introduction & Investment Thesis

When I previously covered Chipotle (NYSE:CMG) in October, I upgraded the stock to a “buy,” as I believed that it was likely at an inflection point, as revenue and earnings growth were expected to pick up pace from Q3 onwards as a result of management’s strategic investments in technological improvements, marketing & digital, and international expansion.

Unfortunately, it was a wrong call on my part.

While revenue did accelerate sequentially in Q3 FY25, it came in far lower than expectations, marking the fifth consecutive quarter of revenue misses. Management attributed the weakness to macroeconomic pressures, particularly on low- to middle-income households.

What further dampened investor sentiment was that management lowered their projections for full-year comparable sales, which are now expected to decline in the single-digit range, as opposed to remaining flat year-over-year. Therefore, instead of revenue and earnings growth picking up pace from Q3 onwards like I (and consensus estimates) had projected earlier, we should brace ourselves for another two quarters at least.

Post earnings, consensus estimates have massively lowered their forward growth expectations, where revenue is not expected to accelerate until Q2 FY26, while earnings per share are expected to decline for the next two quarters.

Without a clear line of recovery in sight, I believe that there is more room for Chipotle to fall from current levels, especially given its current price-to-earnings multiple. As a result, I have downgraded my rating to a “sell” with an updated price target of $27 per share, representing a downside of 20% from current levels.

Missed Revenue Expectations For The 5th Quarter In A Row

Chipotle reported its Q3 FY25 results at the end of October, where revenue fell short of expectations, growing at 7.5% YoY to $3.0B. Not only that, Q3 FY25 also marks the fifth consecutive quarter of revenue miss, which is very disappointing in my opinion. In my previous post, I had written that consensus estimates were pointing to a revenue growth of 9.39% YoY for Q4.

While the year-over-year revenue growth was higher than the previous quarter, which management attributed to new restaurant openings and a 0.3% increase in comparable sales from a 1.1% increase in average check (offset by lower transactions of 0.8%), this was far from the “inflection” that I was expecting.

During the earnings call, management attributed the weakness in revenue to a "broad-based pullback in frequency across all income cohorts" and mentioned that the gap between the low- and middle-income customers has been widening, particularly among the 25- to 35-year-old cohort, which represents 40% of total sales.

In all of this, the management admitted the urgency to move quickly with an actionable plan, as Chipotle stock is down close to 50% from its all-time highs in 2024. On their list of action items, they are upgrading their restaurants with HEAP (high-efficiency equipment package) to improve team experience and throughput and ensure consistency during peak periods. So far, in restaurants where HEAP is live, they are seeing the taste of food and guest satisfaction scores improve along with growing labor efficiency.

Going down their list of action items, they are also spending their effort on menu innovation with initiatives like new sauces and limited-time offers, which “drove a step-up in transactions.” In addition to that, they are looking to expand their catering options as a potential growth lever, while scaling their digital experiences in the form of reward programs to attract and engage their customer base.

Finally, management also discussed their international strategy with upcoming expansions in Europe, the Middle East, and Asia, including a new joint venture partnership in South Korea and Singapore.

The international strategy was detailed with upcoming expansions in Europe, the Middle East, and Asia, including a new joint venture partnership in South Korea and Singapore. Boatwright stated, "In 2026, we anticipate opening between 350 and 370 new restaurants."

Massive Downward Revisions to Forward Growth Rates

Despite management’s efforts to revive growth back into the company, the future is blurry.

First of all, aside from missing revenue estimates in Q3, management lowered their guidance for full-year comparable sales growth, where they now expect it to decline in the low single-digit range, as opposed to their previous guidance, where they expected comparable restaurant sales to remain flat for the year.

Moreover, when looking at forward revenue growth estimates, Chipotle is now expected to grow at just 4.05% and 6.95% YoY in Q4 FY25 and Q1 FY26, respectively, which are lower than Q3 FY25 results. When I last wrote about Chipotle in October, consensus estimates were projecting revenue to start accelerating from Q3 onwards to 9.32% in Q4 FY25 and 10.13% in Q1 FY26, respectively.

The same is true for earnings per share that are now expected to contract for the next two quarters, where consensus expects it to find a bottom in Q1 FY26 before it turns positive. When I last wrote about the stock, earnings per share were expected to accelerate to 16.4% and 13.26% in Q4 FY25 and Q1 FY26, respectively. These are some severe downward revisions we are talking about.

Revisiting my Valuation

Looking forward, I will take the revised consensus estimates into account when assessing my price target.

On the revenue front, Chipotle is now expected to grow its revenues for the full year FY25 by 5.3% YoY (as opposed to the previous estimate of 7.2%). This should be followed by revenue growth recovering to 9.8% and 11.59% YoY over the next two years to $14.6B in revenue by FY27 as pressure from comps eases, along with management’s efforts to revive growth in the company.

When it comes to profitability, earnings per share are now expected to grow just 3.38% YoY (as opposed to 8% in my previous writing), followed by 5.52% and 17.93% YoY over the next two years, respectively. This will total to $1.44 in earnings per share by FY27.

Chipotle stock is currently trading at 30x its projected FY25 earnings. Unfortunately, with its revenue miss in Q3, followed by severe downward revisions to forward guidance, I believe that there is further room for the stock to fall from current levels. The way I see it, it should be trading at par with the 10-year average price-to-earnings multiple of the S&P 500 of 18.8, given that its earnings per share over the next three years will average a little over 9%.

Therefore, assigning a PE ratio of 18.8, we arrive at a price target of $27 per share, which represents a downside risk of 20% from current levels.

My final verdict and conclusions

The inflection that I was expecting at Chipotle’s has definitely been delayed by a couple of quarters at the least. Without a clear sign of recovery, I believe the stock may face further downward pressure given its price-to-earnings multiple. As a result, I am downgrading my rating to a “sell” with a price target of $27 per share, representing a downside of 20% from current levels.

data.title: Vista Gold: The Market Sees A Transformative Future In An Economically Robust Mt Todd
data.description:
A “Buy” Rating for Vista Gold Corp.

Vista Gold Corp. (VGZ), a company based in Englewood, Colorado, which is developing future gold production in Australia, is a Buy in our opinion, consistent with our previous article from May 29, 2025, and with the general market sentiment.

In the “About Vista Gold Corp.” section of Vista Gold's third quarter 2025 financial results (“Q3 2025 Results”) reported by Seeking Alpha, the company describes itself as follows:

Vista holds the Mt Todd gold project, a leading development-stage gold deposit located in the Tier-1 mining jurisdiction of Northern Territory, Australia. The Project offers strong project economics, significant initial production, expansion potential, development optionality, exploration upside, advanced local infrastructure, and broad community support.

Our Update

Since our last Buy rating, shares of Vista Gold Corp. (VGZ), hereinafter "VGZ", listed on the NYSE, have risen by 60.66%, visibly outperforming the US stock market index. At the time of this article, the S&P 500 had gained only 14.97% over the same period. The difference could be even greater, as the S&P 500 only includes the largest publicly traded companies in the US, and its performance in recent years has been heavily influenced by the hype surrounding massive investments in AI.

It wasn't the seductive call of a siren that propelled the stock from its previous level to dizzying heights, but rather a turning point in a transformative future for VGZ Company. Fueled by the desire to solve the puzzle, demand focused on reconciling VGZ Company's decision to scale down the Mount Todd gold project with Mount Todd's projected potential to generate outstanding returns, sending the share price into a meteoric rise. The ability to trigger a price surge is extremely ambitious and still holds considerable potential. With the likely cause of this success now on the verge of being unravelled, it's time to provide an update on our Buy rating.

From Our Previous Article

In our previous article, we expressed our view that a Buy rating was appropriate for investors interested in Vista Gold Corp. via its NYSE-listed shares. We were convinced that these shares would benefit from rising demand, as the Mount Todd gold project was due for a revaluation, with investors considering the increased likelihood of Mt Todd developing into a producing mine with a reduced scale and greater adaptability to the current global economic climate. This was not just any gold mining project, but a highly profitable one with remarkable exploration potential in Australia's mining-friendly Northern Territory. The project was well-secured in terms of infrastructure and local community support. Given the rising gold price, VGZ was therefore ideally positioned for tremendous growth.

Feasibility Study for the Scaled-Down Mt Todd Gold Project

The Mt Todd project – wholly owned by Vista Gold Corp. – is located 40 km northwest of Katherine in the Northern Territory, Australia, and is considered by the company to be one of Australia’s largest gold deposits under development. Gold extraction will be carried out via open-pit mining. According to the company’s revised feasibility study, the project remains economically viable with a capacity of 15,000 tonnes per day (the “2025 FS”). This profitability will have a positive impact on the company over the entire life of the mine, assuming gold prices remain robust. The area also offers very good expansion potential, which should reflect solidity and suggest growth. In order to commence construction of the Mt Todd open-pit gold mine, the various necessary operating and environmental permits are in place. These permits are subject to change due to regulatory requirements arising from the resizing of the project, but VGZ sounds convincing in suggesting that it will not take longer than 18 months (from July 2025) to have things settled with the permits amended as well.

The left side of the slide shows the Mt. Todd gold project before the revision, while the remainder depicts its transformation into a 15,000-tonne-per-day per day capacity feasibility study (5.3 million tonnes per annum).

From our perspective, the scaled-down feasibility study shows that VGZ intends to substantiate its plan to establish gold production in Australia's Northern Territory with facts, and that this will not just be a series of long-standing purposes, unfulfilled promises and enticing figures on slides. With the price of gold continuing its unabated climb to historic highs, VGZ is pursuing accelerated value realization. A revised feasibility study, projecting a production rate of 15,000 tonnes per day, requiring minimal financing and reducing initial investment costs by ≈ (“approximately”) 60%, significantly increases the likelihood of developing the Mt. Todd gold mine.

Frederick H. Earnest, President and CEO of Vista Gold Corp., said (from “2025 FS”):

This Study marks a significant shift in the strategy for Mt Todd, […] incorporating methods to reduce development and operational risks.

As the feasibility study “2025 FS” illustrates, these methods primarily aim to promote “Strong and Stable Gold Production from Well-Defined Deposit ”, according to the company.

The graphic from the company's “2025 FS” illustrates annual gold production over 15 years. After optimising the processing plant in years 2 through 4, stable production volumes are expected from years 5 through 15, preceded by a ramp-up in the first year. With an AISC LOM of US$1,499 per ounce, the company joins the ranks of global competitors, many of whom are stunned by the blows of a high AISC that, with an average of $1,544/oz as the benchmark for all, is already enormously higher than Mt Todd.

And there's more: While the global industry's AISC/oz has risen steadily over time and may continue to rise, Vista Gold promises in its “2025 FS” document that the AISC/oz for the Mt Todd project will be around $1,499/oz for the first 15 years of the project's life and then for a total project life of 30 years.

The Exploration Potential

Given the enormous exploration potential of the surrounding resources and the remaining untapped resources, value creation is expected to continue for a long time, as the gold price, as it currently stands, remains very promising.

The mine shoreline could at any time flood the 35-year production (146,000 oz/year over the entire mining period / proven and probable reserves – 5.2 million oz averaging 0.94 g/t), with an additional 1.8 to 3.5 million ounces of gold in the Measured, Indicated and Inferred Mineral Resource base averaging ≈0.8145 g/t from the 24 km Batman-Driffield trend and 496,000 ounces of gold averaging ≈1.15 g/t from the Quigleys deposit in the Measured and Inferred Mineral Resources. The success of the Batman deposit could extend to the northeast, where equally valuable deposits exist in all directions. This would allow the exploration team to drill and conduct follow-up studies in those areas. It should be noted that upgrading these higher-grade resources to the upper category will improve the quality of future reserves, which should have a positive impact on the business, as reserves will lead to higher returns through improved operational sustainability and better economics. This gives Mt Todd the opportunity to strengthen its cohort in the highly competitive global gold mining market, and shares of VGZ stock on the NYSE will gain extra points.

In addition, the enclave, with mining permits and resources that can be upgraded through exploration, is located within at least 1,337 km² of adjacent licenses to explore for precious and base metal discoveries. These district-level exploration areas could reserve a swarm of impetus for the market value of VGZ shares to continue soaring in the future.

VGZ Vista Gold Corp.'s Market Value

At the time of this writing, shares of Vista Gold Corp., which trade on the NYSE under the symbol “VGZ”, were trading at $1.99 per share, representing a market capitalisation of $247.37 million.

Technical Analysis

The share price is 35.8% above the average of $1.465 per share within its 52-week range of $0.47 to $2.46 per share. The 20-day moving average (20-MA) fell below the 50-day moving average (50-MA) at the beginning of last month, which, following the correction in the gold price from its all-time high in mid-October and the subsequent consolidation, indicated a bearish sentiment. This temporary downward momentum has now weakened, as the 20-MA, driven by the gold price action (last month +5.24% in the Gold Spot Price (XAUUSD:CUR)). It should not be long before the price breaks through the 50-day moving average, confirming a solid uptrend in the short term as well, in addition to the longer-term of the MA-200 Cross. Although the RSI at 61.41x is closer to overbought levels than before, everything suggests that the market cap can still impress. Based on the recent correlation between the stock price recovery and the RSI, the market capitalisation even managed to scale up by 150% (from late August to mid-September), despite the narrow range signalled by the RSI. But we must acknowledge the tailwind from the announcement of the “2025 FS” that we referred to earlier in this analysis. We are confident that providing financing for the construction of the Mt. Todd mine will give substance to this project, pave the way for its transformation, and inject new momentum into the share price.

Valuation

The “2025 FS” claims that Mt Todd has “robust economics”, namely the following:

Although this may not apply to all price ranges, the most likely method for estimating the net present value (NPV) is to assume a linear relationship between the gold price and the after-tax net present value at 5% discount rate. The calculated estimates for the net present value are:

A) ≈ $3.43 billion at the current gold price of $4,196.05 per ounce.

B) ≈ $3.78 billion based on Deutsche Bank's 2026 forecast of $4,450 per ounce.

C) ≈ $4.54 billion based on Deutsche Bank's 2026 forecast of prices around $5,000 per ounce.

Vista Gold has a number of shares outstanding of 126.21 million (see Seeking Alpha here under “ Trading Data”), and if, which is never the case as the company uses as much venture capital as debt capital, they raise $425 million for the construction of the Mt Todd gold mine through the issuance of additional ordinary shares on the stock market, the current volume of shares outstanding would increase to a total of 339.7 million pieces based on the current share price on the NYSE. The calculations for the individual scenarios are as follows:

A) The net present value (NPV) per share is $10.10 at the current gold price.

B) The NPV per share (based on Deutsche Bank's forecast for 2026: $4,450/ounce) is $11.13.

C) The NPV per share (based on Deutsche Bank's forecast for 2026: gold price is around $5,000/ounce) is $13.40.

Vista Gold owns 100% of the Mt Todd project. This means that even in the (unlikely) event of raising all the capital for construction through a stock offering, the potential value of these shares on the NYSE could be 407% to 575% higher than the current share price of $1.99.

Also, Mt Todd's "2025 FS" projects after-tax free cash flow of $1.6 billion for the first 15 years of commercial operation, based on a gold price of $2,500. Mt Todd's average annual after-tax free cash flow is $106.67 million. This puts its market capitalisation-to-after-tax free cash flow ratio at 2.32x, compared to the sector median of 9.98x. Given current gold prices and Deutsche Bank's gold price forecasts, Mt Todd's future cash flow, which is an “after-tax free” measure of cash flow, could be even higher, and to an even more favourable comparison with the sector median.

The Risk Section

VGZ has liquid assets of $13.72 million and is debt-free. Currently, its ongoing business operations use approximately $2 million in liquid assets per quarter. Assuming the company does not raise capital through a share issue – as it has done so far – these liquid assets will last for a maximum of two years.

The company will finance the construction of the mine with debt in addition to equity, which is the most likely scenario. The debt burden on the balance sheet has implications for the sentiment around Vista Gold shares on the NYSE, as the company will then have to divert funds otherwise earmarked for further growth to repay the debt and pay interest, as has been the custom in the industry worldwide since its inception. But it is also true that lenders' lending at a time when credit conditions generally remain tight will further improve sentiment around VGZ shares. Western economies in general, including the Northern Territory Province in Australia, are less credible today than they were before COVID-19. As a sign of the strained economic situation, the Reserve Bank of Australia left its key interest rate unchanged at 3.6% at its November 2025 meeting to combat excessive inflation, which peaked in 2022. Interest rates are still at their highest in over a decade, and policymakers highlighted the continuing uncertainty surrounding the national and global outlook (based on Trading Economics as of this article). In contrast to this scenario, which is not at all conducive to credit provision, as it was before Covid-19 in 2020 and all the other crises that have followed since then, including the energy crisis, wars and inflation, it implies that lenders will see Mt Todd as a valuable asset both now and in the future if VGZ is successful in obtaining loans. The likelihood of this happening is certainly higher now than in the pre-2025 FS period, as technical consultants with proven experience in Australian and international projects have re-sized Vista Gold Corp.'s Mt Todd gold mine project into one that is easier to implement. Assuming they are rational in their view, stock market forces can only be very pleased with this, at least if lenders, despite their cautious stance on a weaker economy, see the value in Mt. Todd to handing over monetary resources to Vista Gold Corp. And perhaps it will become one of the most sought-after stocks if value versus growth wins the tug-of-war amid fears of a potential AI bubble. Just imagine what this unprecedented combination could mean for VGZ stock: a fantastic gold price and investors flocking to value stocks.

With 1.85 million VGZ shares traded on the NYSE, this stock is not very liquid, so having to sell shares to cash in can lead to unpleasant consequences, such as poor performance, delays, or even losses. Conclusion

Vista Gold Corp., a NYSE-listed company headquartered in Englewood, Colorado, USA, is developing the Mt. Todd gold mine in Australia. In our view, the stock is a buy.

The company now plans to build a smaller-scale gold production facility in Australia. Vista Gold has revised its project compared to the original version, reducing initial capital expenditures by nearly 60%. The location offers favourable exploration and mining regulations, enjoys strong local support, and has solid infrastructure. We believe the project now has a better chance of securing financing, as this factor has not yet been addressed for the operations and construction to begin. Should Vista Gold be able to convince investors and, at a later stage, lenders to secure the necessary financing, this would significantly underscore Vista Gold Corp.'s position as a valuable and reliable company, amid a context in which credit conditions remain constrained by global economic uncertainty, and investors are much less inclined than in the past to expose their wealth to the risk in the financial markets.

The experts who advised on the revised Mt. Todd project have demonstrable experience with similar Australian gold mine projects. This aspect, together with the project's robust economic metrics, is no guarantee of success, but the prospects for success are very promising.
